---
title: Distribution
description: Wrap Bub as a product — pin the framework, ship a custom CLI, bundle plugins and skills, and embed BubFramework programmatically.
sidebar:
  order: 5
---

This page shows how to package Bub as the runtime kernel of your own product. The case study throughout is [`visual-base`](https://github.com/oilbeater/visual-base) — a distribution that pins `bub`, ships its own `visual-base` CLI, and bundles two plugins (`bub_kimi` and `bub_eye`) plus a skills package.

## Before you begin

- A plugin or two ready to publish (see [Plugins](/docs/build/plugins/)).
- A `pyproject.toml`-based build backend (`hatchling`, `uv_build`, or `pdm-backend`).

## 1. Pin the Bub kernel

A distribution is, first, a Python package whose dependencies include Bub. Pin a tight range so a new minor release of Bub does not silently change behavior for your users:

```toml
[project]
name = "visual-base"
version = "0.2.0"
dependencies = [
    "bub>=0.3.6,<0.4",
    "loguru>=0.7",
]
```

Bub follows the [stability promise](/docs/build/#stability-promise) — kernel hardened, plugins loose. The `>=0.3.6,<0.4` range opts into patch-level fixes within the `0.3` minor line.

## 2. Ship a custom CLI

The cleanest way to brand a Bub-based product is a thin CLI that defers to Bub's own Typer app. Visual-base's [`cli.py`](https://github.com/oilbeater/visual-base/blob/main/src/visual_base/cli.py) is the reference pattern:

```python
# src/visual_base/cli.py
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import sys

from visual_base.settings import VisualBaseSettings


def _has_explicit_workspace_flag(argv: list[str]) -> bool:
    return any(
        arg == "--workspace" or arg == "-w" or arg.startswith("--workspace=")
        for arg in argv
    )


def main() -> None:
    if not _has_explicit_workspace_flag(sys.argv[1:]):
        workspace = VisualBaseSettings().resolve_workspace()
        workspace.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        os.chdir(workspace)

    from bub.__main__ import app

    app()
```

Two points worth calling out:

- The product resolves a sticky workspace before importing Bub, then `chdir`s into it. [`BubFramework`](https://github.com/bubbuild/bub/blob/main/src/bub/framework.py) snapshots `Path.cwd()` as its workspace at construction time, so the `chdir` must happen first.
- After that, the product hands off to `bub.__main__.app` — the same Typer app the bare `bub` command uses. Every plugin contributing `register_cli_commands` lights up automatically.

Wire the CLI to a script entry point:

```toml
[project.scripts]
visual-base = "visual_base.cli:main"
```

End users now run `visual-base chat` instead of `bub chat`, but get the full pipeline.

## 3. Ship multiple plugins from one distribution

Nothing forces a one-plugin-per-package layout. A distribution can host several plugin modules under one wheel and register each one through its own entry-point key. From visual-base's [`pyproject.toml`](https://github.com/oilbeater/visual-base/blob/main/pyproject.toml):

```toml
[project.entry-points.bub]
kimi = "bub_kimi.plugin"
eye = "bub_eye.plugin:EyeImpl"

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/visual_base", "src/bub_kimi", "src/bub_eye", "src/skills"]
```

`bub_kimi` is a module-shaped plugin (a `run_model` hook that delegates to the Kimi CLI). `bub_eye` is a callable-class plugin that holds a framework reference (a background channel that records the screen). One `uv pip install visual-base` registers both with Bub.

## 4. Ship skills with the distribution

Listing `src/skills` in `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] packages` puts the skills directory on Bub's builtin discovery root. See [Skills → Ship skills with your package](/docs/build/skills/#3-ship-skills-with-your-package) for the full mechanics.

## 5. Programmatic embedding

If your product hosts Bub inside its own process — an HTTP API, a worker, a notebook — skip the CLI and drive [`BubFramework`](https://github.com/bubbuild/bub/blob/main/src/bub/framework.py) directly. An **envelope** is the dict (or message object) that carries one inbound message through the pipeline.

```python
import asyncio

from bub import BubFramework


async def run_one_turn() -> None:
    framework = BubFramework()
    framework.load_hooks()
    inbound = {
        "session_id": "embedded:demo",
        "channel": "embedded",
        "chat_id": "demo",
        "content": "hello from a host process",
    }
    async with framework.running():
        result = await framework.process_inbound(inbound)
    for outbound in result.outbounds:
        print(outbound["content"])


asyncio.run(run_one_turn())
```

`framework.running()` is the async context that resolves [`provide_tape_store`](/docs/build/hooks/#5-provide-a-tape-store) and any other lifecycle-bound hooks. Open it once around the lifetime of your service, not per turn, when running in long-lived processes.
